coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-based antimicrobial photosensitizer: Synthesis, characterization and antimicrobial performance against food borne pathogens
coredata.dc:creator: Divsalar, Elahe
Tajik, Hossein
Moradi, Mehran
Molaei, Rahim
coredata.dc:description: In this study, quasi-spherical carbon dots (CDs) with a white appearance were synthesized from acetate and zinc (Z). The size of the CDs was less than 10 nm and −11.4 mV zeta potential. At concentrations lower than 5 mg/mL, the Z-CDs showed no cytotoxicity in L929 fibroblasts. The antimicrobial activity of Z-CDs was dose-dependent on both Gram-positive (Listeria monocytogenes) and Gram-negative (Salmonella Typhimurium) bacteria in antimicrobial assays. Furthermore, observations from SEM imaging confirmed this activity. The photodynamic properties of the photosensitizer Z-CDs were examined with a focus on Z-CDs concentration and irradiation [i.e., time (0–120 min), temperature (4 and 25 °C), and LED lamps (360 and 420 nm wavelength)]. It was evident that L. monocytogenes was susceptible to photodynamic treatment at 25 °C when both wavelengths were utilized. The photodynamic properties of Z-CDs were influenced largely by temperature, followed by wavelength and Z-CDs concentration parameters. Remarkably, the bacterial cell membranes were significantly destroyed by Z-CDs and photodynamic treatments. These multifunctional Z-CDs are highly appealing candidates for food and biomedical applications owing to their favorable characteristics; such as low toxicity, photosensitizing properties, and appearance.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot-based antimicrobial photosensitizer: Synthesis, characterization and antimicrobial performance against food borne pathogens

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, quasi-spherical carbon dots (CDs) with a white appearance were synthesized from acetate and zinc (Z). The size of the CDs was less than 10 nm and −11.4 mV zeta potential. At concentrations lower than 5 mg/mL, the Z-CDs showed no cytotoxicity in L929 fibroblasts. The antimicrobial activity of Z-CDs was dose-dependent on both Gram-positive (Listeria monocytogenes) and Gram-negative (Salmonella Typhimurium) bacteria in antimicrobial assays. Furthermore, observations from SEM imaging confirmed this activity. The photodynamic properties of the photosensitizer Z-CDs were examined with a focus on Z-CDs concentration and irradiation [i.e., time (0–120 min), temperature (4 and 25 °C), and LED lamps (360 and 420 nm wavelength)]. It was evident that L. monocytogenes was susceptible to photodynamic treatment at 25 °C when both wavelengths were utilized. The photodynamic properties of Z-CDs were influenced largely by temperature, followed by wavelength and Z-CDs concentration parameters. Remarkably, the bacterial cell membranes were significantly destroyed by Z-CDs and photodynamic treatments. These multifunctional Z-CDs are highly appealing candidates for food and biomedical applications owing to their favorable characteristics; such as low toxicity, photosensitizing properties, and appearance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past few years, there has been noticeable interest in the utilization of nanomaterials or nanostructures in food processing, safety, and packaging. This phenomenon can be linked to their exceptional characteristics (Mohammad, Ahmad, Ibrahim, & Zaidi, 2022). Depending on their shape, nanostructures predominantly consist of nanolayers, nanowires or nanorods, nanoholes, nanocapsules, nanoclusters, fullerenes, nanocomposites, nanotubes, colloids, carbon dots (CDs), and nanoparticles (Moradi, Razavi, Omer, Farhangfar, & McClements, 2022). A lot of research has been done on using CDs in the food industry, mainly to create new nanomaterials and tools for making food safer and better in quality (Moradi, Molaei, Kousheh, T.Guimarães, & McClements, 2022). Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon-based nanomaterials with zero dimensions and are smaller than 10 nm in size. They comprise a nanocrystalline core with an amorphous layer (Semeniuk et al., 2019). CDs exhibit remarkable physicochemical, optical, electronic, and biological properties. Their features include low toxicity, exceptional luminescence, high hydrophilicity, thermal stability, chemical inertness, and convenient utilization, owing to their distinct structures and sizes (Peili Li, Han et al., 2020; Y. Wang, Sheng, et al., 2023). The particular characteristics of CDs are contingent on several factors, such as the source of carbon, the chosen synthesis method, and the intended purpose of use. Till now, a multitude of procedures for synthesizing CDs have been established and broadly categorized as either "top-down" or "bottom-up" methods (Ghosal & Ghosh, 2019; Lin et al., 2021). The "bottom-up" method, such as the hydrothermal approach, is typically straightforward and economical, thereby it is a suitable option for large-scale preparation using saccharides, amines, organic acids, and their derivatives as carbon sources (Sharma & Das, 2019). Acetate (C2H3O2) is an ideal and promising carbon source for synthesizing CDs, given its high water solubility, low toxicity, and cost-effectiveness. It was used as an available source without the need to adjust the carbon ratio (G. Gong et al., 2022).

The ultrasmall size and abundant external functional groups of CDs enable them to exhibit antimicrobial properties on diverse microbial species via several mechanisms, such as oxidative stress elicited by reactive oxygen species (ROS), physical and mechanical degradation, inhibition of bacterial metabolism, and photocatalytic activity (Xin et al., 2019). CDs have garnered attention for their particular photophysical characteristics as photosensitizers (PS), which enhance their effectiveness in microbial inactivation (Nie et al., 2020). Photodynamic inactivation (PDI) is a non-thermal technique used to combat pathogens in the presence of light sources, photosensitizing agents, and molecular oxygen, which commonly leads to ROS production (Corrêa et al., 2020; C. Gong et al., 2020). A feasible approach involves the combination of a non-toxic photosensitizer (i.e., CDs) and a light source which operates at a suitable wavelength to facilitate the conversion of light to ROS during PDT. This process can be instrumental in destroying crucial biological molecules and cellular structures, leading to impaired cell function and death (Knoblauch et al., 2021). PDI offers several benefits, including a non-specific impact on intracellular and cell membrane biomolecules and extensive efficacy against planktonic and biofilm forms of microbial species, including those that are antibiotic-resistant (Dong, Liang, Meziani, Sun, & Yang, 2020), low microbial resistance, low energy consumption, and cost, reduced environmental impact, photostability, and high quantum efficiency (Anas et al., 2021).

Doped-CDs (D-CDs) are produced by adding metal or non-metal agents to the CDs to improve and regulate their intrinsic properties, including quantum fluorescence efficiency, catalytic activity, and antimicrobial and antioxidant properties (F. Li, Yang, & Xu, 2019; Miao et al., 2020). Heteroatoms (e.g., nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur, and boron) and metals (e.g., zinc, magnesium, silver, gold, and copper) are among the most common doping agents. The benefits of including metal ions in the CDs framework, as opposed to heteroatoms, are associated with the abundance of catalytically active sites within CDs, superior electron-donating capability, enhanced quantum yield, optical absorption, and physicochemical properties that facilitate the generation of multicolored emissions across several wavelengths (Tejwan et al., 2021). Therefore, using zinc as a doping agent in the matrix of CDs proves highly beneficial for enhancing the properties of these nanomaterials, which includes improving optical detector performance, increasing photocatalytic activity, and enhancing antibacterial properties.

Photodynamic properties of CDs synthesized from citric acid (Nie et al., 2020), riboflavin (Yue et al., 2021), turmeric (Yan et al., 2023), iodine- and nitrogen-doped CDs (X. Wang, Lu, Hua, Yang, & Yang, 2021), and copper-doped CDs (Nichols et al., 2020) have been investigated for different pathogens. Zinc-doped CDs (Z-CDs) are assumed to have increased active sites, enhanced photocatalytic activity, doping efficiency, and antimicrobial performance. Nevertheless, the noteworthy characteristic of Z-CDs is their odorless and colorless nature, which makes them suitable for food applications. In this study, zinc acetate was utilized as a precursor for the production of Z-CDs, which were subsequently characterized. The high hydrophilicity, remarkable fluorescence efficiency, excellent biocompatibility, and optimal antimicrobial photodynamic properties of Z-CDs under blue light irradiation at 360–420 nm warranted their selection as suitable agents for assessing their photodynamic and antimicrobial performance. Our primary focus was to comprehensively analyze the photodynamic properties and mechanisms of the antimicrobial action of Z-CDs, which belong to a new class of photosensitizers with potent antimicrobial properties.

Listeria monocytogenes (PTCC 1298), and Salmonella Typhimurium (PTCC 1709) were obtained from the Iranian Research Organization for Science and Technology (IROST), Tehran, Iran. The Luria Bertani Broth (LB), Tryptic Soy Broth/Agar (TSB/TSA), Mueller Hinton Agar (MHA), and the agar-agar powder were supplied from Quelab (Montreal, Canada). Sterile PTFE syringe filters were supplied from Millipore Inc (Massachusetts, USA), ethanol from Lian Fidar Kia Co, Alborz, Iran, and other chemicals and reagents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). LED lamps (360 and 420 nm) were also acquired from Saba Light (Tehran, Iran).

Z-CDs were synthesized hydrothermally using zinc-acetate solution as the doping agent and carbon source. This was accomplished by preparing a zinc acetate solution (1%), pouring it into a hydrothermal reactor (300 mL), and heating it for 6 h at 200 ± 1 °C. The resulting cooled solution was centrifuged (15000 rpm for 30 min) and filtered via a sterile PTFE syringe filter (0.45 μm) to remove impurities. Eventually, Z-CDs solution was freeze-dried and kept in dark and sterile conditions at 4 ± 1 °C for later use.

The absorbance spectrum in the range of 200–600 nm with a resolution of 1 nm, was measured using a Thermo Scientific NanoDrop spectrophotometer (model 2000 C, Waltham, USA). Additionally, a spectrofluorometric scan was conducted using a JASCO FP2200 fluorescence spectrophotometer from South Korea. The particle size distribution histogram and zeta potential value of the Z-CDs were determined using a Malvern Zetasizer (ZEN 3600 instrument, Malvern Instruments Ltd, UK). Surface morphological investigations were conducted using High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) (Leo 906 E, Carl Zeiss, Germany). Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) technique was used to evaluate the stability of as-prepared Z-CDs (Guo, Zhang, Cao, & Leng, 2016). For this aim, the prepared Z-CDs were stored in a dark place (4 ± 1 °C) for 8 weeks. The fluorescence intensity of Z-CDs was measured once a week using a spectrofluorometric upon excitation at 410 nm.

To investigate the chemical and functional properties of the Z-CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted using a Thermo Nicolet instrument, Nexus® 670 (USA). The measurements were carried out in the infrared range of 4000-450 cm−1, with a resolution of 2 cm−1. The collected data was further analyzed using the Omnic software (version 6.0; Thermo Electron, Madison, WI, USA). A Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker AXS GmbH, Karlsruhe, Germany) was used, employing Cu K radiation with a wavelength of 1.54 Å and a voltage of 40 kV and a Bruker 400 MHz NMR spectrometer (Bruker, Advance 400, Hamburg, Germany) were used to observe the XRD profile and 1H and 13C NMR, respectively. To determine the elemental and chemical compositions of the Z-CDs, XPS was performed using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Berlin, Germany).
The antioxidant activity was assessed using DPPH (Ghorbani, Tajik, Moradi, Molaei, & Alizadeh, 2022) and FRAP (Benzie & Strain, 1999) tests.

The cytotoxicity of Z-CDs on the mouse fibroblast cell line (L929) was assessed using a standard MTT colorimetric assay (Varasteh, Braber, Kraneveld, Garssen, & Fink-Gremmels, 2018). Initially, L929 cells were placed in a 96-well plate containing 100 μL of culture medium and incubated at 37 ± 1 °C for 24 h in a humid environment containing 95% air and 5% CO2 until the cells adhered to the plate. Afterward, the culture medium was removed, and the cells were exposed to various concentrations of Z-CDs (0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, and 10 mg/mL) with 100 μL volume for an additional 24 h. Finally, the culture medium was replaced with an MTT solution (0.5 mg/mL) and further incubated at 37 ± 1 °C for 4 h in a humid environment containing 95% air and 5% CO2. The cell supernatant was then replaced with DMSO after 4 h. Finally, absorbance was measured at 570 nm by an ELISA reader device (BioTek ELx808, USA). The cell viability was calculated using Eq.(2) . DMSO 5% was used as the positive control. A negative control containing DMEM supplemented with 10 μL of FBS was also applied.

The agar well diffusion method was performed as a suitable method to investigate antimicrobial properties. A bacterial inoculum containing 8 log10 CFU/mL of different strains (L. monocytogenes and S. Typhimurium) was spread onto the surface of MHA. Next, 8 mm diameter wells were made with a sterilized punch and filled with 100 μL of various Z-CDs concentrations (25, 50, and 100 mg/mL). Ampicillin (10 μg/mL), gentamicin (10 μg/mL), and chloramphenicol (30 μg/mL) were used as the standard. The zone of inhibition (ZOI) was recorded after 24 h incubation (37 ± 1 °C) (Sun et al., 2021). All procedures were performed under aseptic conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Minimum inhibitory and minimum bactericidal concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: The MIC and MBC of Z-CDs were obtained using the broth dilution method (CLSI, 2012). First, 160 μL LB broth was added to each well of a 96-well microplate. Different concentrations of Z-CDs were added to each well to obtain final concentrations of 1.52, 0.76, 0.38, 0.19, and 0.09 mg/mL. The bacterial suspension (20 μL; 6 log10 CFU/mL) was added to each well and the microplate was incubated at 37 ± 1 °C for 24 h. Finally, MIC, defined as a 2 log10 CFU/mL reduction in the initial bacterial inoculum, and MBC, defined as a 3 log10 CFU/mL reduction in the initial bacterial inoculum, were determined by the colony count method. LB medium served as the blank, LB medium containing bacterial suspension in the absence of Z-CDs was used as a positive control, and LB medium with Z-CDs but without bacterial suspension served as the negative control.

The photodynamic features of Z-CDs were determined according to Nie et al. (Nie et al., 2020) with some modifications. Briefly, Z-CDs at different concentrations (½ MIC, MIC, and 2 MIC) were added to 1 mL of the bacterial suspension (8 log10 CFU/mL) in a 24-well plate and kept in the dark for 15 min. The mixture was then separately irradiated by two LED lamps (360 and 420 nm wavelength; 15 W power; 120 min irradiation time with a distance of 12 cm) at 25 ± 1 °C and 4 ± 1 °C. At 30-min intervals, 100 μL of each suspension was collected, diluted, and spread onto TSA medium using the drop track method, and the plates were incubated at 37 ± 1 °C for 24 h (Naghili et al., 2013). The treatments were divided into four groups: a) suspension without Z-CDs and irradiation, b) suspension without Z-CDs with irradiation, c) suspension containing Z-CDs and without irradiation, d) suspension containing Z-CDs with irradiation.

First, the bacterial strains were incubated in TSB for 18 h at 37 ± 1 °C and adjusted to 6 log10 CFU/mL. In the presence of Z-CDs (1 × MIC concentration), both before and after irradiation with LED lamps (360 and 420 nm, 15 W, irradiation time 120 min at a distance of 12 cm) at temperatures of 25 ± 1 °C and 4 ± 1 °C. The samples were then incubated at 37 ± 1 °C. Similarly, a control sample was prepared without the Z-CDs. After treatment, the bacteria were gathered by centrifuging at 4000 rpm for 10 min and then washed with a phosphate buffer solution (PBS) having a pH of 7. Next, 50 μL of bacterial cells were left to dry. The bacteria that were treated were gathered by spinning them down through centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 10 min. They were then washed with a phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at a pH of 7. Afterward, 50 μL of the bacterial cells were evenly distributed over aluminum foil and left to dry.

The samples were fixed with 50 μL of 2.5% glutaraldehyde at 22 ± 1 °C. After 12 h, bacterial cells were dehydrated by immersion in increasing concentrations of ethanol (50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 95, and 100%, each for 30 min). Finally, the cells were dried, treated with a gold coating, and examined using SEM (JSM-6500F, JEOL Ltd., Akishima, Tokyo, Japan) (Cui et al., 2021).

The fluorescence intensity difference was measured utilizing a fluorescein solution of approximately 0.5 μg/mL which was alkalinized by the addition of 50 μL of 1 M NaOH. The samples were exposed to an LED for 120 min under conditions comparable to those used in the photodynamic test conditions (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, et al., 2022). The Stern-Volmer equation was used to determine the level of ROS, and this was achieved by calibrating with different concentrations of H2O2 (1, 2, 5, 10, 15, and 25 mM) in a solution of fluorescein.

Biofilm inhibition assay was evaluated according to (Park, Mackay, & Reid, 2001; Pennone et al., 2019) with some modifications. In each well of a 96-well microplate, 160 μL of TSB broth, 20 μL of Z-CDs at final concentrations (MIC, 2MIC, and 4MIC), and 20 μL of an 18-h suspension of L. monocytogenes and S. Typhimurium were added to achieve a final concentration of ∼7 log10 CFU/mL in each well. Wells containing only TSB were designated as controls. Subsequently, the microplate was incubated at 37 ± 1 °C for 24, 48, and 72 h, and then the supernatants from each well were carefully aspirated. All wells were washed three times with PBS and then air-dried at ambient conditions. Finally, biofilm cells were recovered in PBS using ultrasonication with 40-kHz water bath (Soltec, Milano, Italy) for 45 s. The recovered cell suspension was serially diluted with PBS, and viable counts were determined.

The experiments conducted in this study were replicated three times and statistical analysis were conducted by GraphPad Prism version 9.01. Additionally, ANOVA was conducted for all of the data collected, followed by Duncan's multiple range test (P < 0.05).

UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrophotometers were used to analyze the optical properties of the synthesized Z-CDs (Fig. 1 a). The UV–Vis spectrum can be considered in three spectral regions corresponding to the carbonic core centers, a shoulder absorption peak in the deep ultraviolet region (>230 nm), and a typical band at ∼ 288 nm, which is linked to the emergence of a π-π* transition from the aromatic sp2 domain. The peak centered at ∼334 nm can be assigned to the n-π* transition of the oxygen-containing groups on the Z-CDs surface. The fluorescence emission spectra of as-synthesized Z-CDs in the excitation wavelength range of 290–470 nm (Fig. 1b) showed an increase in peak intensity without a shift in the peak position. The fact that the emission peak reached a maximum (centered at 430 nm) upon excitation at a wavelength between 410 and 440 nm, and then exhibited a sharp decrease in intensity is worth noting. This outcome is in agreement with the findings of previous studies (Xu et al., 2016, 2018). The quantum yield of Z-CDs was less than that of acetate-CDs (Siahcheshm & Heiden, 2023), reaching only 21.3%. Based on the obtained data, the intensity of the sample progressively decreased, and by the 7th week (Fig. S1), it reached below 80% of its initial level.

The shape, morphology, and crystallinity of Z-CDs were investigated by HR-TEM and Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) patterns. As shown in the TEM micrograph (Fig. 1c), the synthesized Z-CDs were composed of irregular shapes (mainly quasi-spherical) with agglomeration. Moreover, observation of the HR-TEM images confirmed the presence of Z-CDs with varying shapes and sizes, whereas the FFT pattern displayed a significantly high degree of crystallinity of the Z-CDs. The synthesized nanomaterial was found to have an average diameter of approximately 9.8 nm and a zeta potential of −11.4 mV, as demonstrated by the particle size distribution histogram (Fig. 1d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR, 1H NMR, 13C NMR and XPS analysis
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR was utilized to achieve more comprehensive characterization and identification of the functional groups present in the Z-CDs (Fig. 1e). The spectral bands at 3387, 2922, and 2859 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of the O–H groups and C–H bonds in the methyl and methylene groups, respectively. Also, the typical bands at 1587 (stretching of CO groups), 1410 cm−1 (asymmetrical and symmetrical bending of C–H bond), the weak band at 1308 cm−1 (bending of the O–H groups), 1228 cm−1 (in-plane bending of aromatic C–H), and the absorption band located at 1043 cm−1 (C–OH stretching) explain multiple functional groups and the surface chemical structure of Z-CDs. Furthermore, the small vibrations recorded at 737 and 538 cm−1 were attributed to the presence of the Zn–O group, which agrees with prior research (Das et al., 2018).

The carbon atom hybridization, types of chemical environments, and bonding nature of Z-CDs were identified by 1H and 13C NMR at room temperature using resonance frequencies of 250 MHz (Fig. 3). the 13C NMR spectra (Fig. 3B), several types of carbon signals were associated with aliphatic (sp3) carbon atoms [in primary (0–30), secondary (20–45 ppm), tertiary (30–60 ppm), quaternary (30–50 ppm)]. It is worth mentioning that the sp3-hybridized carbons adjacent to oxygenated groups (such as C–O, CH–O, CH2–O) lie in the 40–85 ppm range (Mazur, Vovk, & Tolstoy, 2020). Moreover, resonances related to sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in CO groups were observed at the 170–180 ppm (De & Karak, 2013). In the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 3C), protons attached to sp3-carbon atoms and bonded to oxygenated groups were identified at 1–3 ppm and 3–5 ppm (shoulder signals), respectively (Ghorbani et al., 2022). The weak signal recorded in the 8–9 ppm range was attributed to the presence of aldehyde hydrogens (De & Karak, 2013).

CDs can be either emitters of radical species acting as an oxidant or radical scavengers as an antioxidant. These molecules become a photosensitizer when excited by light. The triplet state enables an energy intersystem crossing with molecular oxygen and the generation of ROS and singlet oxygen. The ability to produce ROS in CDs depends on the wavelength and intensity of the light source in order to provide the necessary conditions to activate ROS (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023). On the other hand, if they are not stimulated by light, they can function as efficient antioxidants (Haida & Hakiman, 2019). DPPH radicals produce a stable complex (DPPH-H) in the presence of hydrogen, which changes the color of the solution from dark purple to pale yellow or colorless (Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2021). The antioxidant activities of various Z-CDs concentrations are illustrated in Fig. S2A. Within the first hour of exposure to the DPPH reagent, the Z-CDs displayed discoloration, which can be attributed to the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of Z-CDs (Ji et al., 2019; Q. Li, Shen, & Xing, 2022). The results are reported here as the IC50 value, indicating the scavenging of 50% of the primary free radicals. The results indicated an IC50 value of 1.14 mg/mL (Fig. S2B), signifying an effective antioxidant capacity that was higher than that of vitamin C (4.34 μg/mL) and BHT (3.8 μg/mL). The scavenging ability of the Z-CDs was dose-dependent, as evidenced by the reduction in DPPH solution absorption at 517 nm and the conversion of the DPPH color from deep violet to yellow, caused by an increase in Z-CDs concentration from 0.01 to 2 mg/mL. This resulted in a proportional increase in the scavenging potential from 23.51% to 68.60%. A decrease in the antioxidant activity of the Z-CDs upon exposure to methanolic DPPH solution was noted, which may be attributed to the production of OH- radicals. Furthermore, the lower solubility and structural integrity of CDs in methanolic DPPH solution, compared to aqueous solutions, decreased their interactions, and hence, the antioxidant inhibition capacity (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, et al., 2022).

As the FRAP test does not measure radical scavenging potential, the results of both the DPPH and FRAP tests are informative in determining the antioxidant capacity of the CDs (Ghorbani et al., 2022). The antioxidant capacity of Z-CDs against FRAP was investigated at concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 10 mg/mL (Fig. S2C), and a total antioxidant capacity of 46 nM of Fe2SO4/L for Z-CDs at 10 mg/mL was demonstrated. This phenomenon can be attributed to the donation of hydrogen atoms from the hydroxyl groups of Z-CDs to the ferric complex. Consequently, the Z-CDs acted as reducing agents in the electron transfer reaction, ultimately resulting in the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+. CDs are categorized into the nonenzymatic radical scavengers’ category and display a large variability regarding free radical scavenging activity with different mechanisms including 1) neutralization ROS by hydrogen donation, and electron transfer mechanism from surface functional groups and 2) formation of radical adducts at sp2 carbon sites. The presence of sp2-rich domains favors the formation of adducts with the radical species (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023). Therefore, the use of CDs as an additive for improving the antioxidant properties of packaging is one of their interesting applications. The 18-fold improvement of antioxidant properties of films containing 2–3 mg/mL CDs has been demonstrated in previous studies (Moradi, Molaei, et al., 2022).

The cytotoxicity of CDs is one of the most critical features affecting their applications. The viability of the L929 cell line was investigated by the MTT assay after 24 h of exposure (Fig. 4 ). Z-CDs concentrations (0.1, 0.5, and 1 mg/mL) resulted in significant cell viability at concentrations between 99% and 74%, whereas an increase in concentration to 10 mg/mL led to a significant reduction in cell viability (p ≤ 0.05). As a result, based on the criteria proposed by Huang and Tang (2021), Z-CDs may be considered non-toxic because of their low cytotoxic effects. Similar findings showed more than 80% viability of L929 cells when exposed to a high concentration of 0.5 mg/mL (Roy, Ezati, Rhim, & Molaei, 2022). The cytotoxicity of CDs is affected by numerous factors, such as the precursor material, size, surface charge and groups, CD concentration, and cell type. For example, L929 cell lines show higher CDs uptake compared to NIH/3T3 cells (Havrdová et al., 2021). The effect of CDs on cell lines may be attributable to the induction of oxidative stress via ROS, which ultimately results in cellular death following damage to the cell structure and composition (Sangeetha, Smriti, Solanki, & Mohanan, 2021). A number of cytotoxicity studies have confirmed the very low toxicity of CDs (Ghorbani et al., 2022; Havrdova et al., 2016).

To acquire an in-depth understanding of the potential antimicrobial activity of CDs, agar well diffusion and microdilution were conducted. The sensitivity of microorganisms to CDs was assessed based on the size of the Zone of Inhibition (ZOI). The average ZOI of the Z-CDs against L. monocytogenes and S. Typhimurium is illustrated in Table 1 and Fig. 5 . Previous studies have established that the susceptibility of nanomaterials in the agar well diffusion method can be classified into four distinct groups (Ghorbani et al., 2022). Consequently, the tested strains demonstrated high sensitivity, moderate sensitivity, and lower sensitivity to Z-CDs at concentrations of 100, 50, and 25 mg/mL, respectively, thereby highlighting the concentration-dependent impact of Z-CDs on their antimicrobial efficacy. There were significant differences (p ≤ 0.05) observed in the ZOI of Z-CDs (100 mg/mL) when tested against L. monocytogenes and S. Typhimurium. The MIC and MBC values of Z-CDs on L. monocytogenes and S. Typhimurium were assessed using the microdilution method. MIC is defined as the minimum concentration of Z-CDs to inhibit bacterial growth, and MBC is the minimum concentration of Z-CDs with a bactericidal effect. The findings revealed that L. monocytogenes exhibited greater susceptibility, as indicated by a MIC/MBC value of 0.38 mg/mL, compared to S. Typhimurium with a MIC/MBC of 0.76 mg/mL, which is due to the difference in the structure of cell wall and interactions between Z-CDs and cells.

Several factors are responsible for the antibacterial performance of CDs. These factors include the type of microbial strains, the concentration of CDs, and inherent properties of CDs, including their particle size, surface functional groups, and charges (Pengfei Li, Shen, & Xing, 2022). Owing to the existence of anionic sites, the bacterial peptidoglycan layer was somewhat porous, thereby facilitating the electrostatic interactions of the CDs. For example, the zeta potential of L. monocytogenes and S. Typhimurium were −2.5 mV (C. Wang, Chang, Yang, & Cui, 2014) and −4.50 mV (Xiong et al., 2020), respectively. Owing to the lower zeta potential of L. monocytogenes, CDs can adhere more easily to L. monocytogenes than S. Typhimurium. Therefore, Z-CDs had less repulsion to L. monocytogenes and S. Typhimurium. As proposed, chemical doping of metals has been identified as a potentially effective means of enhancing the antibacterial efficacy of CDs. The doping process modifies the surface charge and overall characteristics of CDs, thereby influencing their antibacterial activity (Long et al., 2021). Consequently, Zn-doping can positively impact the performance of CDs by increasing the number of active sites and augmenting crucial attributes such as photocatalytic activity, doping efficiency, and bacteriostatic effect. Due to the favorable overlap between the photoluminescence ranges of CD and Zn, there is a probability of energy/charge transfer between CD and Zn. Therefore, in comparison to other reported studies, Zn-doped CDs demonstrate high doping efficiency, resulting in beneficial effects on their bacteriostatic activity against foodborne pathogens (Das et al., 2018). The generation of ROS and the release of Zn2+ ions damage the cell wall, leading to cell death. These metal oxides function as highly effective antibacterial agents due to the production of ROS, which include O2 −, OH−, and H2O2 (Ijaz, Zafar, Islam, Afsheen, & Iqbal, 2020).

The aPDI performance of Z-CDs as photosensitizers were examined for factors related to Z-CDs (concentration) and irradiation (time, temperature, and source of irradiation) (Fig. 6 ). The assay was conducted under irradiation of 15 W for 0–120 min, with an initial bacterial concentration of 8 log10 CFU/mL. L. monocytogenes was sensitive to photodynamic treatment at both irradiations at 25 ± 1 °C. L. monocytogenes was inactivated at 0.76 mg/mL concentration, after 90 min of exposure to 365 nm, while at the same concentration, inactivation occurred after 60 min of exposure to LED420 nm. An 8 log10 CFU/mL reduction occurred at the end of 120 min in the presence of 1.52 mg/mL Z-CDs in S. Typhimurium, while its inactivation occurred at all investigated concentrations with LED420 nm, confirming a more significant effect of LED wavelength compared to PS concentration. The bacterial strain subjected to dark conditions in the control group exhibited an approximate amplification of 1.5 log10 CFU/mL for both microorganisms. Conversely, under treatment conditions at 25 ± 1 °C, the antibacterial performance of Z-CDs was observed, resulting in the inactivation of S. Typhimurium after 90 min of LED irradiation at 365 nm. This effect was observed even when the treatment conditions were modified to 4 ± 1 °C.

The inactivation process was expedited when exposed to a wavelength of 420 nm, indicating the simultaneous effect of temperature and radiation. Moreover, L. monocytogenes exhibited greater sensitivity, with a higher level of inactivation in two investigated spectra at 25 ± 1 °C. In contrast, the control group showed a slower increase at 4 ± 1 °C than at 25 ± 1 °C group (Fig. 6). L. monocytogenes is known for its resistance to environmental stressors, particularly low temperatures. The physical state of bacterial cell membrane lipids plays a crucial role in maintaining membrane fluidity and function. Temperature fluctuations can alter the lipid composition of the membrane, leading to a decrease in fluidity (Gandhi & Chikindas, 2007). When exposed to low temperatures, L. monocytogenes experiences changes in its membrane fatty acid composition, including an increase in unsaturated fatty acids and a reduction in fatty acid chain length. These alterations reduce carbon-carbon interactions within the cell membrane, thereby preserving membrane fluidity, which is essential for growth at low temperatures. Additionally, in response to low temperatures, L. monocytogenes produces cold shock proteins and can accumulate compatible solutes such as glycine, betaine, and carnitine, which are known for their protective effects in cold conditions (Gandhi & Chikindas, 2007). Consequently, due to these factors, L. monocytogenes exhibits higher resistance to irradiation at 4 ± 1 °C compared to S. Typhimurium. According to the graphs in Fig. 6B, S. Typhimurium was completely inhibited by 2MIC under LED420 radiation at 90 min, while it was inhibited at 365 after 120 min (25 ± 1 °C), which indicates an increase in the generation of ROS at 420 radiation. On the other hand, the antimicrobial effects and ROS generation increased due to a lack of resistance at 4 ± 1 °C, in S. Typhimurium.

The Gram-negative bacteria lipopolysaccharide layers play an important role in resisting photodynamic deactivation. However, based on the current study, LED irradiation in the range of 400–460 nm can effectively deactivate microorganisms. This can be attributed to the fact that the relevant spectrum stimulates endogenous porphyrin molecules (Jiang, Scholle, & Ghiladi, 2019). S. Typhimurium has already been shown to be more resistant to 405 nm wavelength than L. monocytogenes (Kim, Mikš-Krajnik, Kumar, Ghate, & Yuk, 2015; Kumar et al., 2015). Su et al. (Su, Yan, Jiang, Li, & Su, 2022) synthesized curcumin CDs by a hydrothermal method and found that under 400–450 nm xenon lamp for 10 min, the bactericidal activity of Cur-NRCDs was reported on E. coli and S. aureus, resulting in their inactivation at concentrations of 10 mM and 15 mM, respectively. This inactivation was achieved by exposing citric acid and ethylene diamine-based CDs to LED light (365 nm) for 1 h (Zhang et al., 2018). The photodynamic technique was employed to investigate the antimicrobial properties of the ZnO/GQD nanocomposite on E. coli during UV365nm exposure, and it was found that antimicrobial effects can occur due to heat rather than photodynamic activity (Liu et al., 2018). Given these circumstances, the present study investigated the impact of photodynamic inactivation under low-temperature conditions.

The ultrasmall size of CDs plays a crucial role in enabling their internalization into cells and their distribution across cellular membranes. Small particles can more readily traverse the lipid bilayer and infiltrate the cytoplasm, thereby enhancing the antimicrobial effects of CDs (Sun et al., 2021). To study how Z-CDs affect bacterial morphology, cell images in the presence or absence of the Z-CDs were captured (Fig. 7 ). Untreated bacterial strains exhibited typical and smooth surfaces, with normal structure, size, and morphology such as rod-shaped L. monocytogenes and S. Typhimurium. Conversely, the treated samples revealed significant destruction of bacterial cell membranes, with surface distortion, surface wrinkles, volume changes, and cell flattening (Fig. 7). In a similar study, morphological changes in Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria were illustrated by CDs hydrogels. The CDs-treated cells were damaged and wrinkled (Cui et al., 2021). More research has indicated that CDs cause changes in the appearance of cell surface structures leading to the release of internal components (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, et al., 2022; Ezati, 2022).

The antimicrobial mechanism of CDs–mediated aPDT includes CDs adherence to the bacterial surface, ROS production, bacterial cell wall/membrane infraction, and oxidative stress excitation with damage to DNA/RNA (Romero et al., 2021; Z. Wang, Sheng, et al., 2023). The bacterial cell surface charge illustrates the penetration of PS into the cellular complexes. The SEM images (Fig. 7) confirmed that when the bacteria were exposed to LEDs (365nm and 420nm), the cell integrity of the two bacteria noticeably disintegrated over 120 min of irradiation. Aggregation, surface deformation, volume reduction, and cell wrinkling strongly suggest that the cell membrane is damaged and that intracellular contents are leaked. Gram-negative bacteria exhibit lower sensitivity to negative photosensitizers (PS) due to their typical net negative charge on the surface at neutral pH. To enhance the inactivation efficiency of Gram-negative bacteria in the presence of anionic or neutral PS, it is necessary to employ higher PS doses, increase irradiation power, and combine neutral or anionic PS with polycationic permeable compounds (Cossu, Ledda, & Cossu, 2021). In accordance with the aforementioned mechanism, physical interactions between CDs and bacteria are the main auxiliary factors that affect photodynamic inactivation. Second, the relationship between CDs and bacterial morphology is crucial. For example, S. aureus has a cluster form; therefore, the cluster could be defended by the bacteria residing inside the cluster from damage by ROS caused by CDs. The interactions and entry of CDs into the clusters are likely to be challenging (Nie et al., 2020). It has been shown that the PS can destroy the normal cell structure of S. aureus and L. monocytogenes and increase cytoplasmic leakage. Genomic DNA destruction is another reason for bactericidal activity (Lai et al., 2021). Oxidative stress is dependent on ROS, which can occur in the presence of light. Following optical excitation, CDs act as a PS, where an electron is transferred from the ground electronic state to an unstable, short-lived singlet excited state with a higher energy level (Cui, Ye, Ping, & Sun, 2020).

The amount of ROS produced in the Z-CDs was evaluated by the ORAC method using a fluorescein solution (Fig. 8 ). The fluorescence values of mixtures containing Z-CDs and fluorescein were measured over time. As the exposure time increased, the rate of absorption of the fluorescence solution containing the Z-CDs declined, leading to a concomitant elevation in ROS production. The amount of ROS generation at different irradiations and times had significant differences (p ≤ 0.05). In contrast, the plain fluorescence solution did not produce any ROS during the entire 120-min time period. According to the functional groups, the formation of ROS; such as O2 −, OH−, and HO2 −; occurs under aqueous conditions. During the interaction between light and PS, electron or energy transfer to molecular oxygen can lead to ROS production and aggressive oxidative action. The ROS generated in excited states cannot be quenched by ROS scavengers, therefore light-activated Z-CDs are uniquely potent antimicrobial agents and generate highly photo-reactive redox species (Dong, Ge et al., 2020). When Zn-based CDs are irradiated with UV or visible light, electron-hole pairs are generated. These photogenerated holes split water molecules into OH− and H+ ions in zinc suspensions. Dissolved oxygen species are reduced to superoxide anions (•O− 2), which react with H+ to produce (HO2 •). Then, they collide with the majority of charge carriers (e−) and hydrogen ions to produce H2O2 molecules, which can penetrate the cytoplasmic membrane and kill bacterial cells (Ijaz et al., 2020). Bacterial cells lack enzymes that modulate enzymes against ROS; thus, the ROS produced may damage numerous intracellular macromolecules, including proteins, unsaturated fatty acids, and genomic structures (Cossu et al., 2021; Di Mascio et al., 2019).

The results indicated that both L. monocytogenes and S. Typhimurium possessed the ability to form biofilms on polystyrene surfaces. Across all treatments, exposure time and concentration were found to have significant effects on biofilm inhibition (Fig. S3). The highest biofilm inhibitory effects, observed at 4 MIC Z-CDs for L. monocytogenes and S. Typhimurium, resulted in a decrease of 3.87 and 2.89 log10 CFU/mL after 72 h, respectively (Fig. S3). CDs had notable biofilm inhibition capabilities attributed to their small size, effectively preventing biofilm formation (Ran et al., 2019). Consequently, CDs represent an exceptionally efficient strategy for combating biofilm-induced health issues. In a study conducted by (Peili Li, Yang et al., 2020), CDs were shown to exhibit strong antibacterial and antibiofilm activity against S. aureus and E. coli, enhancing electrostatic interactions between microorganisms and protonated amines on the CDs surface. Additionally, the extended surface corona-like layer of CDs facilitated their penetration into biofilms.

In this study, a new type of white-appearing zinc-doped CDs was synthesized. These Z-CDs displayed moderate antimicrobial/antioxidant properties along with excellent aPDI capabilities against both Gram-positive bacteria (L. monocytogenes) and Gram-negative bacteria (S. Typhimurium). The results of aPDI experiments revealed that the pathogen inactivation occurs when Z-CDs are exposed to LED irradiation (λ = 420 nm, 15 W, 120 min). SEM observations confirmed that the antimicrobial activity of Z-CDs, a novel class of antimicrobial photosensitizers, is associated with the disruption of the bacterial cell wall. The key factors influencing the aPDI capabilities of CDs appeared to be temperature (25 °C), followed by wavelength (λ = 420 nm), and Z-CDs concentration (0.76 mg/mL). The non-toxic, colorless, and antimicrobial photosensitizer characteristics of the synthesized Z-CDs possess numerous potentials uses within the food matrix, thanks to their antimicrobial and antioxidant attributes. Furthermore, they can serve as nanofillers and provide antibacterial, antioxidant, and UV-blocking functionalities in food packaging materials.
